destination.vibe: A 62,000-person mountain town where Chinese Nationalist soldiers stranded after Mao's 1949 victory planted oolong tea on 1,200-meter ridges and built a Yunnan village that has nothing to do with Thailand — and a 108km road loop climbing 1,700 meters through it waits 40km from your guesthouse, morning clear before the rains arrive.

flights.outbound_dates: July–August flexible (Tue/Wed departures cheapest; book 6–8 weeks out)
flights.notes: DTW → ICN (Seoul Incheon) → BKK (Bangkok Suvarnabhumi). Then add a domestic leg: Thai AirAsia or Nok Air BKK (Don Mueang DMK) → CEI (Chiang Rai), ~$30–45 each way. Book separately at airasia.com or nokair.com. Korean Air accepts bikes as checked baggage ($200 each way) — or fly into CNX (Chiang Mai) instead if CEI pricing is harder; then arrange a 3-hour van transfer to Chiang Rai (~$80 private, ~$15 shared songthaew).

flights.outbound_dates: July–August flexible (midweek Tue/Wed best; book 6–8 weeks out)
flights.notes: DTW → TPE (Taipei Taoyuan) → BKK. EVA is Taiwan's flagship carrier and one of the most comfortable long-haul options to Southeast Asia — good legroom in economy, Taipei connection is smooth and often has a longer layover with lounge access on higher fares. Add domestic BKK → CEI separately (~$30–45). EVA bike policy: accept bikes as checked baggage; fees vary by fare class, typically $75–150 — confirm at evaair.com during booking.

flights.outbound_dates: August (slightly cheaper than July; book 6–8 weeks out)
flights.notes: DTW → HKG (Hong Kong) → BKK. Cathay's Hong Kong hub is one of the great connection airports — large, efficient, multiple lounge options. Add domestic BKK → CEI separately. Bike policy: bikes accepted as checked sports equipment; fees apply (~$100–200 each way depending on route and fare class). Note: July–August is rainy season in Northern Thailand — structures rides for mornings. See tips.

lodging.name: Bed & Bike Hostel (Poshtel)
lodging.neighborhood: Chiang Rai city center — near Clock Tower
lodging.highlights: The only explicitly cycling-dedicated accommodation in Chiang Rai — bike hire, secure bike storage, and a coffee shop/restaurant on-site. Private rooms from ~$25–30/night. Walking distance to the night market and Clock Tower. 3.7 miles from CEI airport. This is the natural base for the Mae Salong and Doi Tung loops: you clip in and roll.

lodging.name: Wiang Inn Hotel
lodging.neighborhood: Chiang Rai city center — 5 min walk to night market
lodging.highlights: The classic mid-range choice for Chiang Rai — well-maintained rooms, outdoor pool, free breakfast included, and free on-site parking (ask at check-in for secure indoor bike storage, which most Thai hotels of this category accommodate without issue). Tripadvisor rating ~4/5. On Phahonyothin Road, the main artery heading north to Mae Chan and the Mae Salong climb.

lodging.name: The Legend Chiang Rai Boutique River Resort & Spa
lodging.neighborhood: Kok River bank — 10 min from city center
lodging.highlights: Boutique bungalow resort on the banks of the Kok River — 78 Northern Thai-style rooms scattered through gardens, with spa, pool, and riverside dining. The most atmospheric sleeping option in Chiang Rai at a price that's still a fraction of a comparable European property. Bike rentals on-site. From $51 in low season, ~$110 in July; listed on Tripadvisor as one of Chiang Rai's top hotels.

itinerary.overview: Chiang Rai sits at 580 meters in Thailand's far north, surrounded by mountain ranges that push to 1,600 meters along the borders with Myanmar and Laos. It's a working provincial town of 62,000, not a resort city — the vibe is closer to a Thai county seat than a tourist destination, which is exactly why it works as a cycling base. The road infrastructure heading north is excellent: two-lane paved highways through rubber plantations, rice paddies, and forest, transitioning to mountain switchbacks as you gain elevation toward the ridges. The cycling culture is real: Chiang Rai hosts stage finishes in the Tour of Thailand and the local road-riding community is active. The one constraint is seasonal: July is the rainy season, which in northern Thailand means reliable morning windows (7am–noon, often clear) followed by afternoon thunderstorms (1–6pm). The rhythm adapts: start early, climb before the clouds build, be descending by noon. The lush green hills in the rain, the tea plantations misting over at dawn, the empty mountain roads on a Tuesday morning — July is not the tourist season here, and that is not a problem.

days.title: Doi Mae Salong Loop — 108km / 67mi, 1,729m climbing
days.morning: Wheels rolling by 7am. Head north from Chiang Rai on Route 1 toward Mae Chan (34km), a flat warm-up through market towns and rice fields. At Mae Chan turn west onto Route 1130 and the climbing begins immediately. The road winds up through rubber plantations, then forest, then the first of the tea gardens — the elevation gain is relentless but the switchbacks are clean asphalt and the views opening behind you are worth the effort. After 25km of climbing you reach Santikhiri, also called Mae Salong: a Chinese Nationalist (KMT) hill town at 1,200 meters, founded by soldiers of the 93rd Division who fled Yunnan after Mao took China in 1949 and spent decades stranded in these mountains before Thailand finally granted them citizenship. The streets are in Chinese characters. People speak Yunnan Mandarin. The oolong tea is grown on terraced hillsides and sold in storefronts that look like a Sichuan market town. There is absolutely nowhere else like it in Southeast Asia. Stop for tea and a bowl of khao soi (northern Thai curry noodles; the local version has a distinct Yunnan Chinese influence) before the descent.
days.afternoon: Descend the south side of Mae Salong via Route 1234 toward Ban Hin Taek — another former KMT village, more recent, less developed, genuinely off the map. The descent is fast and well-surfaced. Continue south through Wiang Pa Pao and hook back east on Route 1150 to close the loop back toward Chiang Rai via Wiang Chai. The last 40km are valley road, flat, efficient. Total time: 5–6 hours riding. Be back in town by 1pm before the afternoon rains. Afternoon: Chiang Rai's Wat Rong Khun (the White Temple) is 13km south of the city and genuinely extraordinary — an entirely white glass-mosaic Buddhist temple built by artist Chalermchai Kositpipat since 1997 with his own money, featuring demons grasping at hands reaching from a sea of skulls below the bridge. Not a joke, actually beautiful. Tuk-tuk from the hotel.
days.evening: Night Bazaar in the city center (open nightly, 6–11pm). The Chiang Rai night market is not the tourist spectacle of Chiang Mai — it's smaller, more local, and genuinely good: Chiang Rai khao soi (richer and more Chinese-influenced than the Chiang Mai version), larb moo (spiced minced pork), sai oua (grilled herbal sausage), and sticky rice eaten with your hands. Chang beer is local to the north. Dinner cost: ~$5–7.

days.title: Doi Tung Climb + Golden Triangle — 95km / 59mi, 1,300m climbing
days.morning: Depart by 6:30am, heading north on Route 110 toward Mae Sai (Thailand's northernmost city, on the Myanmar border, 65km from Chiang Rai). At the Doi Tung junction (35km north), turn west onto the mountain access road. The Doi Tung climb: 18km to 1,389 meters, average gradient 7%, forested switchbacks the entire way. About two-thirds up, the road passes through the Mae Fah Luang Royal Project — the country's most celebrated development success story. In the 1980s, this was a major opium-production zone operated by armed militias and KMT remnants. The late Princess Mother (grandmother of the current king) established her summer villa here and drove a decade-long crop-substitution program, replacing poppy with macadamia, tea, coffee, and cut flowers. The slopes that grew opium now look like a Thai interpretation of a Swiss botanical garden. The Mae Fah Luang Garden (Doi Tung Royal Villa complex, ~200 THB entry) at the summit has 100+ acres of ornamental planting, the teak Royal Villa, and views north across Myanmar. Allow 45 minutes.
days.afternoon: Descend north through Mae Sai border town (chaotic, interesting, worth 20 minutes walking the border market). Continue east along Route 1016 — the Mekong River Road — toward Chiang Saen and the Golden Triangle. The road follows the river at close range; across the water is Myanmar, then Laos as you approach Sop Ruak. The Golden Triangle at Sop Ruak: the literal confluence point where the Mekong splits three countries. The Hall of Opium museum (800 THB, ~2 hours) is one of Southeast Asia's best: a serious institutional examination of the opium trade from 1750 to today, built by the same Royal Project that eradicated opium in these hills. The exhibits are dense, the artifacts extraordinary, the context provided by the view out the windows — you're in the former epicenter — is surreal. Don't skip it.
days.evening: Riverside dinner in Sop Ruak or Chiang Saen watching longtail boats move on the Mekong between three countries at dusk. Return to Chiang Rai by songthaew or arrange a van transfer if you've spent the afternoon at the museum (~60km back, ~1h). Dinner back in the city: Phu Lae restaurant on Trairat Road is the long-standing local institution for northern Thai food — the naem prik noom (green chili dip with vegetables) and larb neua (beef larb) are what you came for.

days.title: Chiang Saen River Loop via Mekong Road — 80km / 50mi, 400m climbing
days.morning: Recovery day in terms of elevation, exploration day in terms of terrain. Depart 7am north on Route 1016. The road rolls through low hills, banana and rubber plantations, roadside markets just opening, the Mekong appearing periodically on your left. Chiang Saen (55km from Chiang Rai): the Lanna Kingdom's former capital, now a quiet riverside town of 5,000. The ancient walls and moat are intact, with temple ruins scattered inside — Wat Chedi Luang (1328, ruined chedi still 58 meters high), Wat Pa Sak with its intact stucco decoration, the small National Museum with Lanna artifacts. The town sees almost no tourists on weekdays. Lock the bike and walk the ruins for 30 minutes.
days.afternoon: Continue 11km along the Mekong to Sop Ruak (Golden Triangle viewpoint again, now in full morning light before crowds arrive if you timed it right) and then ride the river road south back toward Chiang Rai via a different routing through the Mae Chan valley. Alternative extension for strong legs: continue north from Sop Ruak to Chiang Khong (40km, flat, the most isolated stretch of Mekong road), a sleepy border town opposite Laos with a famously atmospheric guesthouse strip — then arrange a van or songthaew back to Chiang Rai (70km, ~$25 private). If you rode hard Day 1 and 2, keep it to the Chiang Saen loop and be home by noon for legitimate rest before the flight home.
days.evening: Last night in Chiang Rai. If the timing works: the weekly Saturday Night Walking Street on Thanalai Road (operates every Saturday evening, 6–11pm, 1.5km of local food, crafts, and hill-tribe vendors). Otherwise, return to whichever night market stall you most regret not ordering more from on Day 1. The Chiang Rai airport (CEI) is compact and efficient — 40-minute taxi from the city center; early morning international connections go through Bangkok.

tips: July rain timing: northern Thailand's rainy season does not mean constant rain. The pattern in July is reliable: mornings clear and cool (mountains at 20–25°C), cloud buildup starting around 11am–noon, afternoon thunderstorms most days 2–6pm. Structure every ride to start by 7am and plan your descent or return by 11:30am. The rains are brief and hard; being on a mountain road descent at 2pm when a storm rolls in is genuinely dangerous. Being back in town by 1pm with a bowl of noodles while it pours is exactly right.

Mae Salong grades: Route 1130 has sections to 22–24% on the steepest ramps. These are short and punchy rather than sustained — a 34-tooth inner chainring and an 11–34 cassette (or larger) make a real difference. The road surface is well-maintained. Clockwise (north side up, south side down via Ban Hin Taek) offers longer but more consistently graded climbing. Counterclockwise is steeper but shorter on the way up.

Bike logistics on the domestic leg: Thai AirAsia (Don Mueang DMK → CEI) and Nok Air accept bikes as checked baggage. AirAsia charges 200–400 THB/segment depending on weight; bike must be in a bag or box with pedals removed, bars turned. Nok Air has similar policies. Book the sports baggage allowance when purchasing your ticket online — adding it at the airport costs more. CEI airport is small; oversize baggage delivers directly in the terminal.

KMT history of Mae Salong: the Nationalist Chinese 93rd Division crossed into Burma after the 1949 Communist victory and spent years fighting and retreating through the Golden Triangle borderlands before the Thai government offered them land in exchange for helping fight Communist insurgents. Their descendants remain — three generations into a stateless history that only recently resolved. The old men in Mae Salong who speak Yunnan Mandarin and play mah-jong in the afternoon have a story that is not written in any guidebook. Ask the tea shop owners.

Hall of Opium (Sop Ruak): this is funded by the Thai royal family and staffed by serious historians. Allow 2 full hours minimum. It covers the chemistry of addiction, the colonial-era trade from India through China, the KMT militias who controlled production here in the 1960s–80s, and the Royal Project's eradication effort. The context of looking out at the actual Golden Triangle hillsides while reading this history makes it unlike any other museum experience you'll have. Entry ~800 THB (~$22).

Hydration and electrolytes: July mountain humidity in northern Thailand is higher than European alpine conditions. Even at 1,200 meters on Mae Salong you'll sweat more than you expect. Carry electrolyte tabs or powder — a Thai 7-Eleven (ubiquitous even in mountain villages) sells Pocari Sweat and coconut water. The village tap water is not reliably safe to drink; buy bottled at every store you pass. Two large bottles minimum for the mountain climbs.
